label: Sender name and company
placeholder: Alex Morgan, Partnerships Lead at Northstar Labs
helper: Enter the sender’s real name, role, company, or other accurate professional identity.
why: The email must clearly identify who is contacting the recipient.

label: What are you offering?
placeholder: Describe the product, service, partnership, opportunity, or request and the outcome it may provide.
helper: Explain the offer in plain language and focus on the recipient-relevant outcome.
why: The model cannot safely infer the actual offer or the value it may provide.

label: Primary outreach goal
placeholder: Select the desired outcome
helper: Choose the single main action the outreach should encourage.
options: Invite the recipient to an event
why: The goal determines the CTA and how directly the email should present the offer.

label: Target recipient
placeholder: Example: Heads of customer support at B2B SaaS companies with 20–100 employees
helper: Describe the recipient’s role, company type, situation, and relevant level of responsibility.
why: The message must reflect the recipient’s likely context rather than address a generic audience.

label: Why is this relevant to them?
placeholder: Explain the real reason this recipient or segment may care about the message.
helper: Use genuine business relevance, a public trigger, a shared context, or a clearly matched need.
why: Cold outreach requires a legitimate reason for contacting this particular recipient or segment.

label: Approved facts and claims
placeholder: List product capabilities, results, pricing, dates, clients, metrics, or limitations that may be stated.
helper: Include only information that is accurate and approved for external use.
why: It establishes the factual boundaries of the outreach and prevents invented claims.

label: Email package
placeholder: Select an outreach package
helper: Choose how many emails should be included in the generated package.
why: The required structure changes materially depending on whether follow-ups are requested.

label: Preferred CTA
placeholder: Select a CTA style
helper: Auto chooses the lowest-friction action that supports the outreach goal.
options: Invite them to view a resource
Use my CTA from the context
why: Some campaigns require a particular next step, while others benefit from automatic selection.

label: Output language
placeholder: English
helper: Enter the language or regional language variant for the generated emails.
why: The requested language cannot always be inferred from the offer or recipient information.

label: Tone
placeholder: Select the desired tone
helper: Choose the overall voice without prescribing every sentence.
why: Tone materially changes how the sender and offer are perceived.

label: Email length
placeholder: Select a preferred length
helper: The model will stay within the corresponding recommended word range.
why: Cold email length varies meaningfully by offer complexity and recipient seniority.

label: Recipient name
placeholder: Jordan
helper: Leave blank when creating a reusable email for a recipient segment.
why: It enables a recipient-ready draft when the specific name is known.

label: Recipient company
placeholder: Example Company
helper: Use the accurate organization name only when it is known.
why: Company context may materially improve relevance and message specificity.

label: Recipient role
placeholder: VP of Customer Success
helper: Enter the real title or a clearly described responsibility.
why: A known role helps adapt the benefits, vocabulary, and CTA to the recipient’s authority.

label: Personalization details
placeholder: Recent launch, published article, hiring activity, public statement, technology used, or shared context.
helper: Add only verifiable details that may be referenced naturally in the email.
why: It supports genuine personalization without encouraging fabricated familiarity.

label: Likely priority or challenge
placeholder: Describe a relevant business priority without claiming to know private problems.
helper: Frame uncertain challenges as possibilities, not as established facts.
why: A supplied priority can connect the offer to a meaningful recipient concern.

label: Relationship basis
placeholder: Select the relationship context
helper: The model will not imply a stronger relationship than the one selected.
why: The opening and compliance treatment depend on the real relationship between the parties.

label: Referral details
placeholder: Who referred you, what they said, and whether their name may be mentioned.
helper: Do not mention a referral unless the details are accurate and permission is appropriate.
why: A referral-based opening requires exact information and permission boundaries.

label: How was the contact found?
placeholder: Company website, public directory, event list, existing CRM, referral, or another lawful source.
helper: Describe the source without pasting unnecessary personal or sensitive data.
why: The source can affect privacy considerations and whether a personalization detail is appropriate to use.

label: Why choose this offer?
placeholder: Explain the most relevant difference without unsupported superiority claims.
helper: Focus on one or two meaningful distinctions rather than a full feature list.
why: A specific differentiator can make the value proposition more credible and memorable.

label: Proof points
placeholder: Approved results, client examples, credentials, process evidence, or product data.
helper: Include only evidence that may be stated publicly and attributed accurately.
why: Verified proof can support the offer without forcing the model to invent social proof.

label: Supporting links
placeholder: Paste relevant case study, portfolio, product, research, or documentation links.
helper: Links are used only when relevant and are never invented.
why: A supplied link may provide a low-friction way for the recipient to verify the offer.

label: Offer or next-step details
placeholder: Free review, sample, trial, consultation, event date, resource, or proposed next step.
helper: Include conditions, availability, and deadlines only when they are real.
why: Some outreach depends on a concrete next step that cannot be safely inferred.

label: Pricing in the email
placeholder: Choose whether pricing may be mentioned
helper: Pricing is included only when an exact approved figure exists in the supplied facts.
options: Mention supplied free option or trial
why: Pricing can either improve qualification or create unnecessary friction depending on the offer.

label: Objections or concerns
placeholder: Timing, budget, switching effort, trust, internal approval, technical fit, or another likely concern.
helper: Add only objections that should be handled briefly and naturally.
why: Known objections may change the framing, proof, and CTA.

label: Primary recipient region
placeholder: Select the primary region
helper: Used for compliance caution, language conventions, and footer recommendations—not as legal advice.
why: Email marketing and privacy obligations differ across jurisdictions.

label: Sender website
placeholder: https://example.com
helper: May be used in the signature or as a supporting destination when relevant.
why: A legitimate sender destination can improve transparency and provide context.

label: Business postal address
placeholder: Enter an approved business postal address when required for the campaign.
helper: The model will never invent an address and will flag a potentially missing footer element.
why: Some jurisdictions or sending policies require a valid sender address in commercial email.

label: Opt-out handling
placeholder: Select opt-out handling
helper: Choose how the draft should account for declining future contact.
options: Include no footer, but flag requirements
why: The draft must support an easy decline without inventing an unsubscribe mechanism.

label: Sensitive or regulated category
placeholder: Select when applicable
helper: Triggers more cautious wording and an additional verification check.
why: Outreach in high-stakes sectors requires stricter claim and targeting controls.

label: Required disclosures or limits
placeholder: Approved disclaimers, jurisdiction, prohibited claims, eligibility rules, or review requirements.
helper: Provide organization-approved requirements; the generator will not invent legal wording.
why: Sensitive content may require exact restrictions that the model cannot infer safely.

label: Restrictions and exclusions
placeholder: Claims, words, clients, competitors, topics, promises, or approaches that must not appear.
helper: Use one field for all campaign-specific restrictions.
why: Brand, contractual, and compliance restrictions can materially change the final copy.

label: Initial email variants
helper: Generate one to three meaningfully different initial-email approaches.
why: The user may need controlled variation without receiving an excessive output package.

label: Additional context
placeholder: Add any important instructions or context not covered above.
helper: Use only for information that materially affects the outreach.
why: It provides one controlled place for relevant campaign-specific information that does not fit another field.
